coredata.dc:title: Designing cellulose nanofibrils/carbon dots intelligent label with colorimetric and fluorescent dual responsiveness for real-time monitoring of food freshness
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Hong
Li, Jian
Cheng, Jun-Hu
coredata.dc:description: This study utilized 1,2,4-triaminobenzene dihydrochloride and NaOH as precursors to prepare the pH optical sensor based on carbon dots (CDs). By incorporating CDs into pineapple peel cellulose nanofibrils (CNF) matrix, an intelligent label (CNF/CDs label) with colorimetric and fluorescent dual responsiveness was created for real-time monitoring of food freshness. The CNF/CDs labels exhibited remarkable sensitivity and recognizability towards pH changes from 1 to 12. They also demonstrated excellent reversibility during acid-alkali cycling. Moreover, these labels exhibited exceptional responsiveness to the alkaline and acidic gas environments formed by ammonium hydroxide and acetic acid solutions, respectively. These responses were visually distinguishable through visible color changes and ultraviolet (UV) fluorescence alterations. Encouragingly, the developed labels were successfully applied to monitor the freshness of prawns and fruits, enabling timely assessment of food freshness levels. The dual-mode response of color and fluorescence provided double assurance for the accuracy of the results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cellulose nanofibers
Carbon dots
Smart tag
pH-responsive
Freshness monitoring

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDs and CDs-based label
Detection of ammonia vapour and acetic acid vapour by CDs-based label
Monitoring of seafood and fruits freshness
Preparation and characterization of CNF/CDs labels
Performance of CNF/CDs labels for detection of ammonia vapour and acetic acid vapour

This research was also supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2023YFD2101002) and the Common Technical Innovation Team of Guangdong Province on Preservation and Logistics of Agricultural Products (2023KJ145).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CDs. (A) TEM morphology image of CDs (the illustration shows the diameter distribution of CDs); (B) FT-IR spectrum and (C) XPS spectrum of CDs; (D) UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs in aqueous solution; (E) Excitation and emission spectra of CDs in aqueous solution; (F) Effects of excitation wavelength on emission spectra.

pH-Responsiveness of CDs. (A) Photographs of CDs solutions with pH value ranging from 1 to 12 under daylight; (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs with pH value ranging from pH 1 to 12; (C) Photographs of CDs solutions with pH value ranging from 1 to 12 under UV light of 365 nm; (D) Emission spectra of CDs solutions as pH increased from 1 to 12 at 448 nm excitation; (E) Proposed mechanism accounting for the pH response property of CDs; (F) The Zeta potentials of CDs solutions with pH value ranging from 1 to 12.

Characterization of CNF/CDs labels. (A) SEM morphology images of CNF/CDs labels (the illustrations are optical photographs of labels); (B) XRD spectra and (C) FT-IR spectra of CNF/CDs labels; (D) Photographs of pH-induced colorimetric change (top) and fluorescence response (bottom) of the CNF/CDs labels; (E) Reversibility of PL emission of CNF/CD15 labels between pH 3 and 9.

Performance of CNF/CDs labels for detection of ammonia vapour and acetic acid vapour. (A) The CNF/CDs labels were subjected to exposure to vapour generated from an ammonium hydroxide solution ranging from 200 to 1000 ppm for 30 min. Photographs were taken under daylight (top) and UV light (bottom) every 5 min; (B) The CNF/CDs labels were subjected to exposure to vapour generated from an acetic acid solution ranging from 0.1 % to 0.5 % (v/v) for 30 min. Photographs were taken under daylight (top) and UV light (bottom) every 5 min. Where 1, 2, 3 and 4 represented CNF/CD1, CNF/CD2, CNF/CD3, and CNF/CD4 labels, respectively.

Freshness monitoring of prawn using the CNF/CDs labels. (A) The photographs of labels under daylight and UV light and the changes of VBN in prawn stored at 4 °C; (B) The photographs of labels under daylight and UV light and the changes of VBN in prawn stored at 25 °C. Where 1, 2, 3 and 4 represented CNF/CD1, CNF/CD2, CNF/CD3, and CNF/CD4 labels, respectively.

Freshness monitoring of red globe grape and strawberry using the CNF/CDs labels. (A) The photographs of labels under daylight and UV light in red globe grape stored at 25 °C and changes of red globe grape stored for 3 days; (B) The photographs of labels under daylight and UV light in strawberry stored at 25 °C and changes of strawberry stored for 3 days. Where 1, 2, 3 and 4 represented CNF/CD1, CNF/CD2, CNF/CD3, and CNF/CD4 labels, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Designing cellulose nanofibrils/carbon dots intelligent label with colorimetric and fluorescent dual responsiveness for real-time monitoring of food freshness

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food Science and Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510641, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food Science and Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510641, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Academy of Contemporary Food Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou Higher Education Mega Centre, Guangzhou 510006, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Academy of Contemporary Food Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou Higher Education Mega Centre, Guangzhou 510006, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Food Science and Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510641, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed to the work equally and should be regarded as co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study utilized 1,2,4-triaminobenzene dihydrochloride and NaOH as precursors to prepare the pH optical sensor based on carbon dots (CDs). By incorporating CDs into pineapple peel cellulose nanofibrils (CNF) matrix, an intelligent label (CNF/CDs label) with colorimetric and fluorescent dual responsiveness was created for real-time monitoring of food freshness. The CNF/CDs labels exhibited remarkable sensitivity and recognizability towards pH changes from 1 to 12. They also demonstrated excellent reversibility during acid-alkali cycling. Moreover, these labels exhibited exceptional responsiveness to the alkaline and acidic gas environments formed by ammonium hydroxide and acetic acid solutions, respectively. These responses were visually distinguishable through visible color changes and ultraviolet (UV) fluorescence alterations. Encouragingly, the developed labels were successfully applied to monitor the freshness of prawns and fruits, enabling timely assessment of food freshness levels. The dual-mode response of color and fluorescence provided double assurance for the accuracy of the results.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food waste is a significant global challenge from various perspectives, including social, economic, and others [1]. One-third of the total food produced worldwide is lost or wasted annually due to spoilage during transportation and/or storage [2,3]. This wastage not only results in economic losses but also causes tens of millions of illnesses and hundreds of thousands of deaths each year due to the consumption of spoiled food [4]. As people's living standards improve, they are increasingly concerned about the quality and safety of the food. Intelligent packaging or smart packaging, an upgraded food packaging type, can help address this issue by providing information on food freshness, temperature fluctuations, and delivery history through the use of indicators, sensors, and data carriers [5,6]. Among the various indicators used to monitor food freshness, the pH indicator is particularly noteworthy. It can provide consumers with real-time information on food quality and has several advantages, including high sensitivity, low cost, ease of use, and non-destructiveness [1,6]. The main principle of a pH indicator is that for meat and seafood, proteins decompose under the action of enzymes and microorganisms, producing alkaline volatile amines that increase the pH of the packaging headspace [1]. For fruits and vegetables, the continuous release of CO2 and volatile organic acids can lead to a decrease in the pH value of the packaging environment during the quality decline process [7]. By monitoring the pH value, the decomposition of food can be determined, providing information on food freshness. Currently, various pH indicator dyes or their modified counterparts have been immobilized into specific matrix as intelligent labels for monitoring the freshness of seafood, meat, fruits, and vegetables [1,2,6–8]. However, there are some shortcomings in these labels. For example, when monitoring the freshness of prawn, most of these labels change color or fluorescence only when the prawn is visibly red and rotten. In reality, the prawn begins to rot before it turns red [1,6]. Additionally, the commonly used natural pigment pH indicators, are unstable, and the extraction process is difficult, while synthetic dyes pose safety risks. Therefore, the development of a suitable intelligent label to monitor food freshness is still important.

Carbon dots (CDs) are optical pH sensors that are easy to synthesize, high sensitivity, exhibit stable photoluminescence (PL), reversible behavior, low toxicity and are environmental friendliness [9–12]. They have large extinction coefficients and high fluorescence quantum yields [13,14]. Currently, there have been reports on the application of CDs-based labels in food freshness monitoring. For instance, Qin et al. [15] synthesized histamine-responsive CDs-based sensors for visually monitoring fish spoilage. Zhang et al. [16] constructed a volatile base nitrogen (VBN)-responsive dual-emissive hydrophobic CDs-based tag for evaluating the prawn freshness. Lin et al. [17] developed a chitosan/N-doped CDs film that was based on pH-mediated fluorescent sensing for monitoring pork freshness. However, these sensors were limited to monitoring meat or seafood, and cannot be used for monitoring fruits or vegetables. Additionally, most of these reported CDs sensors were based on a single-mode response signal, making the monitoring results easily disturbed by environmental factors and leading to insufficient sensitivity and low accuracy [1,18]. The dual-mode indicator can provide two distinct signals for self-validation and self-correction, enhancing the reliability of the information regarding the freshness of packaged food. Wang et al. [13] and Yang et al. [14] independently synthesized colorimetric and fluorescent dual responsiveness CDs by hydrothermally treating 1,2,4-triaminobenzene dihydrochloride as a precursor. These CDs were respectively used for detecting Cu2+ and monitoring wound pH value. The constructed sensing platform demonstrated a dual-mode response to changes in pH. Despite of these advantages, the development of dual-mode responsive biopolymer labels utilizing pH-sensitive CDs with both colorimetric and fluorescent responses as sensors for monitoring food freshness has not been widely studied. Cellulose nanofibrils (CNF) are engineered nanomaterials derived from cellulose. They can be sourced from a variety of materials, including cotton, wood, jute, and agricultural waste such as rice straw, bagasse, and pineapple peel residue [19–22]. CNF has a high aspect ratio, large surface area, high mechanical properties, thermal stability, and biocompatibility [23]. It has been widely used as a substrate material for biomimetic materials, flexible electronic devices, food packaging, medical supplies, and more [24–26].

Therefore, this study aims to develop a colorimetric and fluorescent dual responsiveness intelligent CNF-based label that can recognize the freshness of both seafood and fruits by naked eyes. The specific work was as follows: (1) preparing a pH optical sensor based on CDs using 1,2,4-triaminobenzene dihydrochloride and NaOH as the precursors; (2) developing an efficient intelligent label with colorimetric and fluorescent dual responsiveness by embedding CDs in pineapple peel CNF; (3) evaluating the acid-alkaline gas response behavior of the resulting labels by exposing them to the alkaline and acidic gas environments generated from ammonium hydroxide solution and acetic acid solution; (4) applying the obtained CNF/CDs label for real-time monitoring of the freshness of seafood and fruits.

Pineapple peel residue waste, prawn (Fenneropenaeus chinensis), red globe grape (Vitis vinifera), and strawberry (Fragaria ananassa Duch.) were obtained from a local vendor (Guangzhou, China). Pineapple peel CNF was prepared based on our previous study [20]. 1,2,4-triaminobenzene dihydrochloride was supplied from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and ammonium hydroxide solution (25 %–28 %) were obtained by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Acetic acid was purchased from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Hydrochloric acid (HCl) was supplied from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory (Guangzhou, China). All chemical agents were of analytical grade.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs and CDs-based label
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs were prepared using the method described by Wang et al. [13] with minor modifications. Specifically, 0.10 g of 1,2,4-triaminobenzene dihydrochloride and 1.00 mL of NaOH solution (1 M) were dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water and subjected to ultrasonic-bath treatment for 10 min. The resulting solution was then transferred to a 50 mL PTFE-lined autoclave for the hydrothermal reaction (180 °C, 12 h). After the reaction, the resulting suspension was filtered through a microporous membrane (0.22 μm) to remove any insoluble carbon products. The solid CDs were obtained using vacuum freeze-drying conditions for further characterization.

The CDs-based label was prepared by vacuum filtering a 30 mL of suspension consisting of 1 mL of 1 % CNF, CDs solution (0.3, 0.2, 0.1, and 0.05 mL, respectively), and ultrapure water. The resulting gel cake was dried by vacuum freeze-drying. The resulting CDs-based labels were labeled as CNF/CD1, CNF/CD2, CNF/CD3, and CNF/CD4, respectively.

The morphology images were captured using a Talos L120c transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) and a field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Zeiss Merlin, Carl Zeiss AG, Oberkochen, Germany). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured with a FT-IR spectrometer (Nicolet iS50, Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., Waltham, MA, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey was obtained on an EscaLab Xi XPS (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). UV–vis absorption spectra were taken by a spectrophotometer (UV-1800, Shimadzu Co., Tokyo, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were obtained using a fluorophotometer (RF-6000, Shimadzu Co., Tokyo, Japan). The visible light and fluorescent images (at 365 nm UV light) were taken using a smartphone (P30, Huawei Technologies Co. Ltd., China). Zeta potential was measured by a Zetasizer Nano (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK). Various solutions with pH levels ranging from 1 to 12 were adjusted using 0.1 M HCl and NaOH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ammonia vapour and acetic acid vapour by CDs-based label
ce:section.ce:para: For ammonia vapour detection, commercially available ammonium hydroxide solution was diluted with ultrapure water to obtain various concentrations (200, 400, 600, 800, and 1000 ppm). Then, 1 mL of each concentration of ammonium hydroxide solution was added to a culture dish with a diameter of 60 mm, and labels were placed inside the cover of the culture dish. The visible light and fluorescence images of those labels were taken within 30 min at 5-min intervals.

For acetic acid vapour detection, the labels were first treated with a 0.01 % NaOH solution and dried to obtain sensitive and response acidic labels. Commercial acetic acid was then diluted with ultrapure water to obtain various concentrations (0.1 %, 0.2 %, 0.3 %, 0.4 %, and 0.5 %). Each concentration of acetic acid solution, totaling 1 mL, was added to the culture dish with a diameter of 60 mm, along with the labels placed inside the cover of the culture dish. The visible light and fluorescence images of those labels were taken within 30 min at 5-min intervals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring of seafood and fruits freshness
ce:section.ce:para: For monitoring the freshness of prawns, the labels were placed together with the prawns in a sealed culture dish (150 mm diameter) and stored at 4 °C and 25 °C, respectively. The photos of the visible light color and fluorescence changes of the labels were taken using a smartphone. During the storage process, the VBN content of the prawns was determined according to the method described in the National Standard of the People's Republic of China GB 5009.228–2016.

For monitoring the freshness of grapes and strawberries, dried labels with 0.01 % NaOH were placed together with the fruits in a sealed container and stored at 25 °C. The visible light color and fluorescence changes of the labels were captured using a smartphone.

All measurements were conducted in triplicate. The obtained results were analyzed using Origin64 software (OriginLab Co., Northampton, USA) and ImageJ_v1.8.0 software (National Institutes of Health, Maryland, USA).

CDs were synthesized through a one-pot hydrothermal method using 1,2,4-triaminobenzene dihydrochloride and NaOH as the raw materials. In this synthesis, 1,2,4-triaminobenzene dihydrochloride served as both the carbon and nitrogen sources, while NaOH acted as a catalyst for the molecular fusion of 1,2,4-triaminobenzene dihydrochloride and also introduced oxygen doping. The TEM image revealed that the obtained CDs were predominantly spherical (Fig. 1A), with a size distribution ranging from 1 to 3 nm and an average size of 1.97 nm (the illustration in Fig. 1A). FT-IR analysis indicated that the CDs exhibited abundant functional groups (Fig. 1B). The two broad absorption bands observed at approximately 3430 and 3220 cm−1 could be attributed to the stretching and bending vibrations of OH and NH, respectively [27,28]. The peaks at approximately 1629 and 1139 cm−1 corresponded to the vibrations of CO and C-OH, respectively [29,30]. Additionally, stretching vibrations of CC at approximately 1390 cm−1 and CN at approximately 1510 cm−1 were also observed [13,14]. The surface chemical composition of CDs was determined by XPS, as shown in Fig. 1C. The XPS full-scan spectrum clearly detected signals of C1s (284.8 eV), N1s (398.6 eV), O1s (531.4 eV), and Cl2p (199.4 eV), indicating that the synthesized CDs were primarily composed of C, N, O, and Cl elements. Furthermore, a peak at approximately 497 eV could possibly corresponded to the KL1 Auger peak of sodium. Further analysis was conducted on the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (Fig. S1) and N1s (Fig. S2) in CDs. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s could be deconvoluted into four characteristic peaks, including CC (284.65 eV), CN (285.3 eV), and COOH (288.3 eV) [14], and the peak at 283.40 eV might represent graphite C. The N1s spectra revealed two nitrogen species of pyridinic N (398.50 eV) and amino N (400.14 eV) [14].

The optical properties of the CDs were evaluated by examining their UV–Vis absorption, PL excitation and emission spectra of the CDs in aqueous solution. Fig. 1D displays the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the CDs, a shoulder peak typically observed at around 263 nm, which could be attributed to the π → π* transition of the aromatic rings in the CDs framework [31,32]. Additionally, a broad peak observed at around 444 nm in the CDs was likely caused by defect states resulting from surface functional groups of the CDs [14,33,34]. Fig. 1E shows that the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of CDs were located at 448 nm and 599 nm, respectively. The emission spectra of CDs in aqueous solution are shown in Fig. 1F. Clearly, the fluorescence spectra demonstrated excitation-dependent behavior, with a red shift in the emission center observed as the excitation wavelength increased from 380 to 520 nm. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity initially increased and then decreased. Similar excitation-dependent behavior was also observed in the fluorescence spectra of biomass-derived CDs from lignocellulose [31].

The pH responsiveness of CDs is crucial for their potential applications in intelligent sensing or detection. Therefore, relevant response behavior was evaluated under different pH stimuli (pH = 1–12). As shown in Fig. 2A, a significant color transition was observed in the CDs solution under visible light as the pH changed from acidic to alkaline. The color changed from dark red to red, then to orange-red, further progressing to orange-yellow, and finally to yellow. Moreover, the colorimetric properties of the pH indicator are demonstrated in Fig. 2B. The absorption band in the ultraviolet region showed minimal changes in response to the pH value. However, the broad absorption band in the visible region exhibited a notable blue shift starting at pH 7. In addition to the colorimetric response to pH, the unique fluorescence sensitivity of CDs was evaluated through the investigation of pH-related emission spectra. When observed under 365 nm UV light, the fluorescence of the CDs solution transitioned from dark red to orange-yellow, and the fluorescence gradually turned on (Fig. 2C). Moreover, as the pH increased, the fluorescence intensity initially increased and then decreased, and the emission spectra underwent a blue shift in an alkaline environment (Fig. 2D). This change could be attributed to the variable ionization of functional groups within the CDs framework in response to changes in pH, which subsequently influenced the degree of deprotonation, leading to alterations in fluorescent properties [31,35]. In an alkaline environment, it was likely that the OH and COOH functional groups on the surface of the CDs were converted into O− and COO− (Fig. 2E) [13,36]. Furthermore, Zeta potential measurements revealed the change in surface charge of CDs from positive to negative as the solution transitions from acidic to alkaline (Fig. 2F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of CNF/CDs labels
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned in the method described above, the CNF/CDs labels were obtained by mixing CDs solution into a CNF suspension, followed by vacuum filtration and freeze-drying (Fig. S3). The hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl groups on the surface of the CDs could interact with the hydroxyl groups on the surface of the CNF via hydrogen bonding and van der Waals forces, promoting the formation and deposition of a composite layer consisting of CDs and CNF onto the filter membrane. The resulting CNF/CDs labels displayed varying degrees of red color, which corresponded to different levels of CDs content (the illustration in Fig. 3A). Fig. 3A displayed the surface morphology images of the CNF/CDs labels. The surface of the blank group without CDs was dense and compact. However, an increase in CDs content resulted in the appearance of defects, such as pores, on the surface of the CNF/CDs labels. This phenomenon could be attributed to the formation of a staggered network structure between the CDs and CNF within the CNF/CDs labels during freeze-drying, resulting in increased porosity and specific surface area of the labels. The increased specific surface area could potentially enhance the labels' response to acidic and alkaline gases. Fig. 3B shows the crystal structure of the CNF/CDs labels. The XRD spectra of CNF/CDs labels exhibited no significant changes compared to that of the pure CNF label, both showing the typical cellulose I crystal structure. This suggested that the presence of CDs did not alter the crystal structure of the pure CNF label. This finding was similar to previous studies [37,38]. To analyze the functional groups present in the labels and investigate the interaction between the CDs and CNF, FT-IR spectra were conducted (Fig. 3C). The broad and strong peak observed at 3000–3800 cm−1 corresponded to the OH stretching vibration. The characteristic peak at around 2940 cm−1 could be attributed to the stretching vibration of CH of hydrocarbon groups in methyl and methylene groups. Two other absorption peaks were observed at 1600–1700 cm−1 and 1000–1300 cm−1, which were probably linked to CO stretching vibration groups in carbonyl groups and CO stretching vibration groups [22]. The FT-IR spectra of the CDs/CNF labels and the blank label were similar, showing no significant differences. This similarity could be attributed to the formation of non-covalent bonds or physical interactions between the CDs and CNF. It was also possible that the quantity of added CDs was minimal, or that the characteristic peaks of the CDs were obscured by the peaks of the CNF [1,2].

Additional evaluations were conducted to assess the responsiveness of the labels in different pH solutions. Fig. 3D displays the changes of the labels under visible light and UV light after being dropped into solutions with varying pH values. Similar to the behavior observed with CDs in aqueous solution, the labels also exhibited significant responsiveness to different pH solutions. When observed under visible light, the blank group without CDs showed no change as the pH value increased from 1 to 12. However, the CNF/CD1 label, which had the lowest CDs content, transitioned from pink to light pink and then to light yellow. The CNF/CD2, CNF/CD3, and CNF/CD4 labels, containing higher CDs content, generally exhibited a clear trend of color change from dark red to red, then to orange-yellow, and finally turning yellow. Meanwhile, under UV light observation, the fluorescence of the CNF/CDs labels underwent a transition from a dark red to an orange-yellow as the pH value increased, gradually intensifying. Notably, as depicted in Fig. 3E, the CNF/CDs label exhibited high reversibility in both its fluorescence response and pH-induced color changes when subjected to cyclic pH switching between pH 3 and 9. This wide pH range of color and fluorescence variation not only enabled the label to be reused multiple times but also allowed for the adjustment of colorimetric and fluorescent properties based on the specific characteristics of the monitored samples. As a result, this label has great potential for widespread application in food monitoring.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of CNF/CDs labels for detection of ammonia vapour and acetic acid vapour
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practical application potential of the label in food monitoring, an ammonia hydroxide solution was used as a model target to investigate the response behavior of the label towards alkaline gases. As shown in Fig. 4A, the CNF/CDs label exhibited a dependence on the concentration of ammonium hydroxide solution and the response time to an ammonia gas atmosphere. Overall, with an increase in the concentration of the ammonium hydroxide solution, the color of the labels changed from pink to earthy yellow when observed under visible light, accompanied by an enhancement in fluorescence intensity under UV light. For the same concentration of ammonium hydroxide solution, there was only a slight change in the labels when exposed to an ammonia gas atmosphere of lower concentration ammonium hydroxide solution (200 and 400 ppm) within a 30-min exposure period. In the ammonia gas atmosphere of higher concentration ammonium hydroxide solution (600, 800 and 1000 ppm), there was no significant change in the color of the CNF/CD1 label with the lowest CDs content when observed under visible light. Nevertheless, upon UV light observation, the initially observed purple fluorescence gradually faded and transformed into yellow fluorescence. Although the fluorescence intensity increased over time, the response to ammonia gas within the 30-min duration was not notably strong. It was noteworthy that CNF/CDs labels with a higher CDs content (CNF/CD2, CNF/CD3, and CNF/CD4 labels) exhibited a more pronounced responsiveness to ammonia gas. When observed under visible light, the labels changed color from pink to an earthy yellow as the response time to ammonia gas increased. Under UV light observation, the initially dark red fluorescence gradually transitioned to orange-yellow fluorescence, showing a significant increase in fluorescence intensity.

Furthermore, a 0.01 % (w/v) NaOH solution was employed to treat the labels, rendering them sensitive to acids. To evaluate their response to acidic gases, acetic acid was selected as the representative target, and the corresponding results are presented in Fig. 4B. Similarly, the CNF/CDs labels also exhibited a dependence on the concentration of acetic acid solution and the response time in an acetic acid gas atmosphere. With an increase in the concentration of acetic acid, the labels transitioned from yellow to a light pink when observed under visible light, while simultaneously exhibiting a weakening fluorescence under UV light. In solutions of acetic acid with the same concentration, CNF/CDs labels with higher CDs content underwent a gradual color change from yellow to light pink when observed under visible light as the response time increased in the acetic acid atmosphere. Meanwhile, when subjected to UV light, the initially bright yellow fluorescence gradually dimmed, becoming significantly darker.

In summary, these results underscore the exceptional sensitivity and responsiveness of the CNF/CDs labels to both alkaline and acidic gases. Consequently, these labels have significant potential for applications in food monitoring.

The potential application of CNF/CDs labels for monitoring the freshness of prawns was evaluated. As depicted in Fig. 5 , the labels were sealed in a culture dish containing fresh prawns and stored at two different temperatures, 4 °C and 25 °C, to monitor the changes in prawn freshness over time. When observed under visible light, all CNF/CDs labels initially appeared pink, except for CNF/CD1 label with the lowest CDs content. When observed under UV light, they exhibited a dark red fluorescence. As the storage time of prawns increased, the labels, except for CNF/CD1 label, exhibited a gradual color shift when observed under visible light. Initially pink, the labels gradually transitioned to a yellowish-pink and eventually turned completely yellow. When observed under UV light, the labels underwent a transition from dark red fluorescence to dark yellow-red fluorescence, and finally settled into an orange-yellow fluorescence, with the fluorescence gradually intensifying over time. Furthermore, the VBN content of the prawns was determined [16], and a certain relationship was established between the changes in the labels and the VBN content. As shown in Fig. 5A, when stored at 4 °C, the VBN content of fresh prawns was 6.48 mg/100 g, which increased to 9.94 mg/100 g after 1 day of storage. This increase indicated that the prawns were still fresh. The labels with higher CDs content (CNF/CD2, CNF/CD3, and CNF/CD4 labels) retained pink under visible light and exhibited dark red fluorescence under UV light, thereby reflecting the freshness of the prawns. After being stored for 2 days, the VBN content increased to 18.26 mg/100 g, causing the corresponding labels to change to a yellowish-pink color under visible light, while exhibiting a dark yellow-red fluorescence under UV light. These changes indicated that the prawns had entered a secondary state of freshness. Nevertheless, after storing the prawns for a period of 3 days or longer, the VBN content exceeded 20 mg/100 g. Notably, the labels underwent a color change, turning yellow when observed under visible light. Furthermore, under UV light, the labels exhibited an orange-yellow, along with heightened fluorescence. These alterations indicated that the prawns had started decomposing and gradually became spoiled, losing their freshness.

For prawns stored at 25 °C (Fig. 5B), the relationship between the changes in VBN content and labels alterations was similar to the changes observed at 4 °C. The initial VBN content in fresh prawns was 5.99 mg/100 g, which increased to 8.88 mg/100 g after 2 h of storage. Labels containing higher CDs content (CNF/CD2, CNF/CD3, and CNF/CD4 labels) retained a pink when observed under visible light and a dark red fluorescence under UV light, indicating the freshness of the prawns. Following 4 and 6 h of storage, the VBN content increased to 11.98 and 16.48 mg/100 g, respectively, causing the corresponding labels to transition to a yellow-pink color under visible light, accompanied by a dark red-yellow fluorescence under UV light. These changes signified the secondary freshness in the prawns. After storage time of 8 h or longer, the VBN content exceeded 20 mg/100 g, leading to the labels taking on a yellow color under visible light and an orange-yellow fluorescence under UV light, emitting a more intense fluorescence. These alterations indicated the initiation of decomposition and gradual spoilage in the prawns, ultimately resulting in the loss of their freshness.

These results indicated that the prepared colorimetric and fluorescent dual-responsive CNF/CDs labels have the potential to serve as intelligent labels for effectively and intuitively determining the real-time freshness of seafood.

To create acid-sensitive labels for monitoring fruits freshness, a 0.01 % (w/v) NaOH solution was employed to treat the labels. As shown in Fig. 6 , the obtained CNF/CDs labels were sealed in a storage container with the fruits samples. The container was then maintained at a temperature of 25 °C to monitor the progression of the fruits over time. Apart from the CNF/CD1 label, all other CNF/CDs labels initially exhibited light yellow color under visible light and emitted orange-yellow fluorescence under UV light. During the first two days of grapes storage (Fig. 6A), the fluorescence of the labels only slightly weakened over time. However, starting from the third day of storage, mold began to appear on the grapes, causing the corresponding labels to appear slightly reddish under visible light and orange-red under UV light, accompanied by significantly reduced fluorescence. Similar changes were observed on the CNF/CDs labels of strawberries during storage (Fig. 6B). However, more pronounced mold growth was observed on the strawberries after three days of storage, resulting in a more significant decrease in fluorescence under UV light. These findings indicated that the prepared CNF/CDs labels exhibited dual responsiveness through color and fluorescence changes, enabling them to determine fruits freshness. This was mainly due to the fact that fruits produce carbon dioxide and volatile organic acids such as lactic acid and acetic acid during respiration or surface microbial metabolism throughout storage, leading to a decrease in pH in the packaging environment as fruits quality deteriorates. This change in pH can be detected by the CNF/CDs labels.

This study synthesized a CDs-based optical sensor with excellent pH sensitivity using 1,2,4-triaminobenzene dihydrochloride and NaOH as precursors. The sensor was then embedded in pineapple peel CNF to construct an intelligent label (CNF/CDs label) capable of colorimetric and fluorescent dual responses. In acidic environments, the labels turned red, and the fluorescence was quenched, while in alkaline environments, they turned yellow, and the fluorescence was activated. Additionally, the labels exhibited high reversibility in acid-alkali environment changes, allowing for multiple reuses and adjustment of colorimetric and fluorescent properties based on the differences in the actual monitored samples. This expanded their application range in food monitoring. More importantly, the high responsiveness of the labels in simulated acidic and alkaline atmospheres enabled its successful application in monitoring the freshness of various food types such as prawns, grapes, and strawberries. The labels demonstrated the best performance in monitoring prawns freshness, with the labels of higher CDs content (CNF/CD3 and CNF/CD4) being the most distinguishable during the monitoring process. However, since assessing fruit freshness requires consideration of multiple aspects including appearance, surface condition, odor, and microbial contamination, future adjustments of the initial colorimetric and fluorescence properties of the labels according to fruit types are needed to achieve more comprehensive and accurate freshness assessment capabilities.

Hong Zhu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Jian Li: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Jun-Hu Cheng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32272466, U23A20267) for their supports. This research was also supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2023YFD2101002) and the Common Technical Innovation Team of Guangdong Province on Preservation and Logistics of Agricultural Products (2023KJ145).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2024.132642.
